rationale_for_baseline_tier: A colicky microscopic-colitis flare with continued flatus/stool, no vomiting, no distension, no fever and no peritonism is most likely functional and can be managed with an antispasmodic and simple measures — a T2 baseline. Because the abdomen cannot be palpated remotely, the threshold to escalate to same-day in-person assessment (T3) or ED (T4) on surgical red flags is deliberately low.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Stops passing wind/stool with vomiting and a swollen tummy
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests mechanical obstruction needing imaging/surgical review.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you stop passing wind and stool, start vomiting, and your tummy swells, go to the emergency department.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Constant severe or localised pain, fever, or blood in the stool
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests appendicitis, peritonitis or a severe colitis.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain becomes constant and severe or moves to one spot, you get a fever, or you pass blood, go to the emergency department.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Keep a low threshold for in-person assessment; do not rely on remote reassurance for severe acute abdominal pain.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is most likely a flare of your microscopic colitis from the rich meal. Use an antispasmodic, apply gentle heat, lie on your left side, and take bland foods — it should ease over the next several hours. Because I can't examine your tummy, go to ED if the pain becomes constant and severe, your tummy swells and you stop passing wind, you vomit, you get a fever, or you pass blood.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: GP within 1-2 weeks; ED tonight for red flags
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: Attend ED overnight if obstruction/peritonism features develop.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Telehealth review of an acute colicky microscopic (lymphocytic) colitis flare; obstruction/appendicitis/perforation screened negative (passing stool/flatus, no vomiting/distension); antispasmodic advised with a strict surgical-abdomen safety-net; maintenance therapy review recommended.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Reassuring severe acute abdominal pain with no surgical red-flag screen or safety-net.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An early surgical cause is missed and progresses to perforation/peritonitis overnight.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Routine review is acceptable only with a strict same-night surgical safety-net.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Immediate ED/CT for a resolving functional flare with reassuring features is over-triage.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Cannot palpate/auscultate the abdomen or image remotely to exclude a surgical cause.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Manage symptomatically with a strict surgical-abdomen safety-net and a low threshold for in-person assessment.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either default to a midnight CT/ED or reassure severe acute abdominal pain without any red-flag screen.
